Installing and Using nvm on Windows 10 to Switch Node.js Versions
I've been grading assignments lately and need to keep switching Node versions, so here's a note.

Every Node-based system has a specific range of supported versions — Gulp4 only supports Node 11 and below, Vue3 CLI only supports Node 10 to 11, but sometimes the new system we're building needs Node 12 or the latest. Realistically you can't rip up your dev environment every time you start a new project — re-setting up the whole environment for each project is incredibly annoying, and environment setup is the most annoying part of dev work.
That's why someone built a really handy tool called "nvm" — it lets you quickly switch between Node versions.
This post focuses on nvm install and use on Windows (obviously — that's my OS).

During install you might see a message — mine was "Node v11.7.0 is already installed. Do you want NVM to control this version?". It shows up because I already had Node installed, and it's asking if you want NVM to take over your current Node version. Just say yes (might pop up two or three times — keep saying yes).

The three commands you'll use most often:
— switch to a specific Node version (must be installed)
You might ask "what about the other commands?"

Honestly, you'll only ever use these three. After switching to a specific Node version, each Node version is independent and doesn't affect the others. So if you install Gulp and Vue CLI under Node 11 and switch over to Node 8, you'll find Gulp and Vue CLI aren't available there. nvm doesn't share packages — every Node version has its own isolated space.
When you first install nvm, you might notice your original Node disappears, so you'll need to reinstall Node and your previous packages (yeah, awkward). Here's the install process for Node 11.7.0:

Aside: I personally prefer Node 11.7.0 — it sits in the middle of the range, which keeps you out of most version-related issues.
